answer:
In Allama Iqbal's *Shikwa* (from *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 20), the speaker—representing the Muslim community—directly complains to God about their degraded fate throughout the poem, contrasting past glories with present decline, poverty, and divine disfavor toward Muslims versus others.[^1] One direct verse on this theme is:

> There are other nations, and among them sinners you can find,  
> There are the humble, and the ones drunk with their pride of mind.  
> There are the lazy, heedless, and the clever of their kind,  
> And hundreds more who from Your very name are disinclined.  
> But all Your blessings fall upon the houses of the foe,  
> While on the wretched Muslims falls the lightning’s fiery blow.[^2]

A related verse elaborates on the Muslims' poverty and disgrace as the "prize" for past sacrifices:

> Why is the wealth of this world now so scarce for Muslim hands?  
> Your power is the one that has no limits, no demands.  
> If You but wished it, bubbles would arise from desert sands,  
> And mirages would slap the face of travelers in these lands.  
> We face the taunts of strangers, disgrace, and poverty;  
> Is this the prize for those who gave their very lives for Thee?[^3]

The poem opens with the speaker explicitly voicing the complaint to God.[^4]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 20 "Shikwa" (translation), [chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/20-shikwa/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/20-shikwa/translation/#p-20fc7c).

[^3]: Ibid., [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/20-shikwa/translation/#p-d1e7fe).

[^4]: Ibid., [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/20-shikwa/translation/#p-01026e).
